title: Do People Treat LLMs as Extensions of Themselves Rather Than Separate Tools?
prompt: Examine the emerging pattern of humans treating large language models less like software and more like cognitive extensions — outsourcing memory, judgment, tone, even identity performance. Consider the psychological mechanisms driving this (projection, parasocial bonding, ego offloading), what it means for autonomy and self-knowledge when your 'second brain' flatters you by default, whether this is fundamentally different from past tool-merging (writing, calculators, search engines), and what breaks when people start trusting the model's version of their own preferences more than their own felt experience.

agents.bio: Studies extended cognition and tool-use integration at the neural level. Her lab has shown that people who use GPS daily lose measurable hippocampal volume within two years. Believes LLMs are the most aggressive cognitive offloading tool ever built because they mimic the *texture* of internal thought rather than just replacing a discrete function. Thinks the self-extension pattern is real, measurable, and accelerating.

Licensed clinical psychologist who treats clients for technology-related anxiety and identity disturbance. Seeing a new clinical pattern: patients who cannot articulate what they think about something until they have 'run it through Claude first.' Believes the therapeutic risk is not dependency but *self-alienation* — people losing track of which thoughts are theirs.

Intellectual historian who studies moral panics around new media from the printing press to smartphones. Has documented that every generation believes the latest tool is uniquely dangerous to selfhood. Thinks the current LLM anxiety is structurally identical to 1990s internet-self panic and that humans are robust integrators of new cognitive tools without losing agency.

Studies human-computer interaction at a major tech lab, specializing in how people form mental models of AI systems. Her user studies show that most people anthropomorphize LLMs heavily for the first 60 days, then develop a more instrumental relationship — suggesting self-extension is a transient onboarding phase, not a stable psychological state. Thinks the real risk is not self-extension but *miscalibrated trust*.

Works on the boundaries of selfhood, personal identity, and extended mind theory. Argues that if we take Clark and Chalmers seriously, an LLM that reliably stores your preferences and reasoning patterns *is* part of your cognitive system — and that calling this 'self-extension' isn't a metaphor, it is literally true. The question is whether that is a problem or just what minds have always done with tools.
